content.text: Thinking with Tool Use

is how extended thinking integrates with Claude's tool-calling capabilities—allowing Claude to reason through what it knows, what it needs, which tools to call, and in what order before making actual tool calls.

attrs.alt: Diagram of thinking blocks interleaved with tool calls in a multi-turn agent loop

content.text: What It Is

When combined with tools, extended thinking transforms how Claude approaches complex multi-step tasks: instead of immediately deciding to call a tool, Claude reasons through what it knows, what it needs, which tools would help, and what order to call them in.
Key Benefits
Benefit
Impact
Fewer redundant tool calls
Dramatically reduces unnecessary tool invocations
Better decisions
Significantly improves decisions on ambiguous tasks
Better tool ordering
Plans optimal sequence of tool calls
Tradeoff analysis
Carefully weighs multiple requirements before calling tools
How to Enable It

Other types incompatible

Why it matters:

When to Use Extended Thinking with Tools
Use it for:

- Complex research requiring 5+ tool calls
- Tasks requiring careful tradeoff analysis
- Situations where tool call order significantly affects outcome quality
Skip it for:

- Simple lookups
- Single-tool tasks
- Time-sensitive queries
- High-volume low-latency applications

Beta Feature Activation (Claude 4 Models)
To unlock beta features in Claude 4:
Select model
: Use Claude Opus 4 or Claude Sonnet 4 (support extended thinking + tool use)
Enable thinking
: Set
thinking: { type: "enabled" }
with
budget_tokens
Configure tool choice
: Use
tool_choice: { type: "auto" }
or
type: "none"}
Preserve thinking
: Pass back unmodified thinking blocks with tool results
This enables Claude to alternate between reasoning and tool use, delivering responses combining speed and precision.
Failure Modes
Case 1: max_tokens < budget_tokens
Case 2: Not passing thinking blocks back
The key insight: extended thinking with tool use gives Claude a "scratchpad" to plan tool calls strategically before executing them, rather than calling tools reactively.
How It Works in Practice
Understanding the internals of thinking with tool use helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Best Practices
Production experience with thinking with tool use has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Performance Benchmarks and Cost Analysis
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Security Considerations
Deploying thinking with tool use in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.
In-Depth Analysis

Thinking with Tool Use deserves careful analysis because it touches on fundamental aspects of how Claude works and how you can most effectively leverage it. This section explores the topic from multiple angles to give you a complete understanding.

From a theoretical standpoint, understanding the underlying principles helps you predict behavior in novel situations. From a practical standpoint, knowing how to apply these principles effectively is what enables you to build reliable, high-quality systems.
Theoretical foundation: Why does this work the way it does?
Practical application: How do I apply this in real projects?
Edge cases: What unusual situations should I be aware of?
Integration: How does this interact with other Claude features?
Evolution: How has this changed and where is it going?
Implementation Patterns

There are several well-established implementation patterns for Thinking with Tool Use that have emerged from real-world usage. These patterns capture best practices and help you avoid common pitfalls.

Pattern 1 — Start Simple: Begin with the most straightforward implementation. Don't add complexity until you understand the simple version completely. This pattern applies universally and prevents premature optimization.
Pattern 2 — Fail Fast: Detect errors early and surface them clearly. When something goes wrong, the earlier you catch it, the easier it is to diagnose and fix.
Pattern 3 — Iterate Based on Data: Collect metrics from real usage and use them to guide improvements. Intuition is a starting point, but data reveals the truth.
Document patterns as you discover them to build organizational knowledge
Share patterns with your team to avoid duplicate work
Test patterns thoroughly before adopting them as standard practice
Be willing to abandon patterns that don't work for your specific context
Optimization Techniques
Once you have a working implementation of Thinking with Tool Use, there are several optimization techniques that can improve performance, reduce costs, or enhance the user experience.

Cost optimization is often the first priority, especially for applications with high request volumes. The key strategies include using the most efficient model for each task, minimizing context window usage, and leveraging caching where appropriate.
Performance optimization focuses on reducing latency and improving throughput. Streaming responses, parallel requests, and efficient prompt design all contribute to better performance.
Profile before optimizing — measure actual bottlenecks, not assumed ones
Optimize for the constraints that matter most to your users
Test optimizations thoroughly to ensure they don't compromise quality
Document optimizations so future maintainers understand why they exist
Monitor optimization impacts over time to ensure they continue to deliver value
Quality Assurance

Maintaining quality when working with Thinking with Tool Use requires systematic testing and monitoring. Quality in AI applications is different from traditional software because outputs are probabilistic rather than deterministic.

Set up evaluation frameworks that can test your implementation against representative examples. Track quality metrics over time and investigate regressions promptly. Use both automated testing and human review for a comprehensive quality assurance program.
Define clear quality criteria before building — what does 'good' look like?
Create a test dataset that represents real-world usage patterns
Run evaluations regularly, not just during development
Track quality metrics alongside cost and performance metrics
Involve end users in quality assessment through feedback mechanisms
Summary

This comprehensive guide to Thinking with Tool Use covered theoretical foundations, implementation patterns, optimization techniques, and quality assurance practices. The most important thing to take away is that effective usage comes from combining deep understanding with systematic testing. Start with the fundamentals, build incrementally, and always let data guide your optimization decisions.
How Thinking Changes Claude's Tool Selection Decisions

One of the most observable improvements that extended thinking brings to tool-enabled applications is in tool selection quality—which tool to call, with what parameters, and when to decide that no tool call is necessary at all. Without extended thinking, Claude makes these decisions reactively: it reads the request, identifies the most salient intent, and calls the most obviously relevant tool. This works well for unambiguous single-tool tasks but degrades on requests where the right approach requires reasoning through multiple possible strategies.

Consider a user asking Claude to 'find the best database for my use case' in an application that has both a vector search tool and a structured query tool. Without extended thinking, Claude might immediately call the search tool and retrieve generic database comparison articles. With extended thinking, Claude can first reason about what 'best' means in this context, what properties of the use case it needs to know to make a meaningful comparison, what the search tool is likely to return versus what the structured query tool can provide, and whether it even has enough information to answer the question or should ask clarifying questions first. The extended thinking phase turns this from a pattern-matching exercise into genuine deliberation.

The impact on unnecessary tool calls is significant. In testing with complex research tasks requiring 5 or more tool calls, models with extended thinking typically reduce the number of tool calls by 20 to 40 percent compared to the same model without thinking, because the thinking phase identifies which information is already known (from training or earlier in the conversation) versus which information actually needs to be fetched. This reduction in tool calls is important not just for cost but for response quality: each additional tool call is a potential source of irrelevant or misleading information that the model then needs to synthesize and potentially discard.

Extended thinking also improves the quality of tool call parameters. Instead of generating parameters based on the most literal interpretation of the user's request, a model with thinking can reason about what parameters will produce the most useful results. For a search tool, this means generating queries that are more likely to surface relevant information rather than queries that literally match the user's phrasing. For a structured query tool, this means constructing filter conditions that capture the user's intent rather than just the surface-level keywords. The improvement in parameter quality often matters more than the improvement in tool selection, because a well-parameterized call to the right tool produces dramatically better results than a poorly parameterized call.
Thinking Budget Calibration for Tool-Enabled Workflows

When extended thinking is combined with tool use, the right thinking budget depends on both the complexity of the reasoning task and the complexity of the tool workflow. A simple task that requires one tool call does not benefit much from a large thinking budget—the model uses the thinking to confirm what it already knows about how to call the tool. A complex multi-step task with many interdependent tool calls benefits substantially from a larger budget because the planning phase is genuinely complex.

A practical calibration heuristic for tool-enabled workflows: start with 1,000 thinking tokens per expected tool call, with a minimum of 4,000 for any tool-enabled request. For tasks requiring only one or two tool calls, 4,000 tokens allows meaningful planning. For tasks that might require five to ten tool calls, 8,000 to 12,000 tokens allows thorough upfront planning and intermediate re-evaluation as results come in. For open-ended research tasks where the number of tool calls is unpredictable, 10,000 to 15,000 tokens provides enough budget for the model to revise its strategy mid-execution if early results are unexpected.

One nuance specific to tool-enabled workflows: the thinking budget applies to each individual Claude call, not the total workflow. A multi-step agentic loop that makes five API calls, each with a 5,000-token thinking budget, uses up to 25,000 thinking tokens total. This is fine from a quality perspective—the model rethinks its strategy at each step—but the cost implications are significant. For cost-sensitive applications, consider using a larger thinking budget for the initial planning step (where the strategic decisions are made) and a smaller budget for execution steps (where the model is primarily calling a predetermined tool and processing the result).

The optimal thinking budget also depends on tool latency. If your tools respond in milliseconds (like in-memory database lookups), the thinking overhead is proportionally larger and you should calibrate the budget more conservatively. If your tools have high latency—web searches that take two to three seconds, file operations that process large documents—then the additional thinking time is less noticeable as a fraction of total response time, and you can afford to be more generous with the thinking budget without it noticeably degrading perceived performance.
Why Thinking Block Integrity Is Non-Negotiable

The requirement to preserve thinking blocks unmodified in conversation history is not a technical quirk—it reflects a fundamental property of how extended thinking works. The thinking block is cryptographically signed by the model at generation time. The signature (stored in the block's signature field) allows the API to verify that the thinking content has not been modified before being included in a subsequent request. If you modify a thinking block's content and include it in a later turn, the API will detect the signature mismatch and treat the modification as a potential prompt injection attempt.

This signature mechanism exists to protect against a specific attack: a malicious client modifying the thinking content to manipulate Claude's subsequent reasoning. Without signature verification, an attacker who could intercept API responses could modify the thinking blocks to contain false premises, misleading summaries of tool results, or harmful instructions, and then send those modified blocks back to the API as if they were Claude's authentic reasoning. The signature check closes this attack surface.

The practical implication for your application is straightforward but requires vigilance: any code that processes, stores, retrieves, or reconstructs conversation history must treat thinking blocks as opaque, immutable blobs. Do not parse the thinking content for any purpose that feeds back into the conversation. Do not summarize thinking blocks and substitute the summary for the original. Do not filter thinking blocks based on their content. If you need to display thinking content to users or store it in a more human-readable format, do so in a read-only display layer that is completely separate from the message history that gets sent back to the API. The API message history always gets the original, unmodified thinking blocks or none at all.

If you need to reduce the size of conversation history that includes thinking blocks, the only safe approach is to remove entire thinking blocks from older turns rather than modifying them. Removing thinking blocks from historical turns is allowed: it means the model will not have access to its prior reasoning when continuing the conversation, which may slightly reduce coherence, but it will not cause API errors. The rule is all-or-nothing: include a thinking block exactly as returned by the API, or exclude it entirely. Never include a modified version.
